schools.summary: 'Ahab' stands for his whole house/posterity; the address 'Jezreel' is an ancient error for 'Samaria'; the nobles were guardians of the royal wards.
Gill: the seventy may include grandsons, since such are called sons in Scripture.
Henry: seventy sons, Gideon's number, could not shield a vicious house from divine vengeance; Jehu's letter is a taunting challenge.

debates.question: To whom were the letters sent, 'the rulers of Jezreel' or of Samaria?

sides.position: an ancient error; read 'Samaria' with the LXX and Josephus

schools.summary: Only the letter's gist is reported; its careful enumeration of the nobles' resources is mocking irony aimed at a luxurious, cowardly aristocracy.
Gill: 'your master's sons' are the sons and grandsons of Ahab shut up in the capital.
'Your master' is Ahab, and 'his father's throne' is Ahab's; the order to 'fight' declares Jehu's warlike intent and forces a choice between hopeless resistance and submission.
Gill: the nobles are bidden to choose the son fittest for government by age and qualifications.

themes: the dare to enthrone a rival

schools.summary: The Hebrew doubles the fear ('mightily, mightily'); 'the two kings' (Joram and Ahaziah) is emphatic, and the nobles reason from their fall to their own helplessness.
Gill: they were intimidated at once, seeing that any resistance would bring Jehu's forces upon them.
The two officers are the palace-prefect and the city-governor, the 'rulers' of v1; the message is an unconditional surrender.
Clarke: they made no conditions and so stood pledged in advance to commit the murders Jehu would command; Wesley identifies the two officers.
Gill: 'he that was over the house' had charge of the royal palace in Samaria.
The 'cool cynicism' of the order and its urgent one-day deadline aim to prove to Jezreel that no prince survives and that Samaria has joined Jehu; the letters were sent from Jezreel.
Gill: having gained his point by the first letter, Jehu turns the nobles' submission into an instrument of slaughter.
The narrator's blunt 'butchered' shows he did not sympathise with Jehu's bloodshed; his real interest is that Elijah's word was fulfilled.
The deed is righteous as God's command yet wicked as the Samaritans' terrified, godless obedience; Clarke recoils at their cold blood.
Gill: they complied at once, without the least hesitation.

debates.question: How can the same slaughter be both righteous and wicked?

sides.position: righteous as the fulfilment of God's command, wicked as the executioners' godless, self-interested obedience
the narrator simply refuses to admire it, centring on the fulfilment of Elijah's word

schools.summary: The heads are displayed at the gate, the place of public business; Ellicott compares Persian custom and traitors' heads on London Bridge.
Clarke: left at the public gate overnight to strike all the people with terror and convince them resistance is vain.
Gill: the messenger likely came from the nobles of Samaria to report obedience.

themes: the two heaps at the gate

schools.summary: Jehu, sitting as judge in the gateway, feigns astonishment ('who slew all these?') and pleads prophecy; the phrase 'Ye be righteous' is genuinely disputed.
Gill: the people were gathered to the shocking sight of the heads.

sides.position: 'ye are guiltless, so have nothing to dread' (reassurance)
'ye are just and will judge justly'
'Are ye righteous?' — making the people share the guilt

schools.summary: Not one syllable of Elijah's prophecy shall fall 'to the earth' as a dead thing; the emphatic clause stresses that Jehovah himself has done it.
Gill: all was brought about by God's providence per prediction, a just punishment on Ahab's house, with Jehu and the nobles as mere instruments.

themes: the word of the Lord not falling

schools.summary: The slain fall into classes: high officials, intimates ('his known ones'), and court priests; the extermination is total, leaving none remaining.
Clarke: the nobles who murdered the seventy sons themselves fell victim to the ambition of 'this incomparably bad man.'
Gill: the massacre was the very design of Jehu's justifying speech.
Beth-eqed-haroim is the 'shepherds' binding-/meeting-house'; the odd 'came and went' marks that the killing happened on the road, not at Samaria; Eusebius's site is judged too far off the route.
Gill: Jehu goes to Samaria to make a clear riddance of Ahab's house and abolish idolatry there too.
The 'brethren' are Ahaziah's kinsmen (his own brothers already slain by Arabs); coming on a courtesy visit, they are caught unaware by Jehu's headlong speed.
Gill: 'brethren' means his brothers' sons, as Lot is called Abraham's brother.
They were slain at the cistern of Betheked for their connection with Ahab's house; the 'two and forty' parallels 2 Kings 2:24 and may be a round number.
Gill: Jehu bade his guards seize them alive as captives before killing them.
Jehonadab founds the Rechabites, an ascetic society for the true religion; Jehu wants to be seen allied with a man revered for sanctity.
Henry: 'Is thine heart right?' is a question every soul should ask; yet Jehu's craving to be seen with a saint may betray self-interest.
Jehonadab, a pious Kenite, comes to encourage Jehu; the handclasp seals their compact and lends Jehu public esteem.
Henry: parading his 'zeal' to be seen, like the Pharisees, betrays a heart set on his own advancement, not on God's acceptance.
Keil: judged objectively the coming slaughter of Baal's worshippers accorded with the law and the theocratic principle, whatever Jehu's motive.
Gill: the boast savours of vain glory, hypocrisy, and a pharisaical spirit.

themes: a right deed in a wrong spirit

schools.summary: 'Ahab' stands for his house; the destruction is total ('till he had destroyed him') and expressly fulfils the word spoken to Elijah.
Gill: Jehu slew all connected with Ahab by blood or by civil and religious office.
Wesley: the manifestly false words cannot be excused, for we must not do the least evil that good may come; Clarke describes the trap without defending it.
Ellicott: a grim double entendre, not a plain lie — Jehu will indeed 'serve Baal much' by slaughtering his worshippers.
Henry: spoken to deceive, it cannot be justified, for the truth of God needs no man's lie.
Gill: Jehu gathered all the people, at least the chief of them.

debates.question: Can Jehu's declaration 'Jehu shall serve Baal much' be justified?

sides.position: no — a manifestly false deception, and no good end excuses a lie
it is a grim double meaning, true to his real intent of slaughtering the Baalites

schools.summary: The convocation deliberately mirrors Elijah's on Carmel; 'in subtilty' is a rare word linked to Jacob the supplanter, rendered by the LXX as a tripping-up.
Clarke: the death-threat, framed as zeal for Baal, was 'done in the very spirit of deceit.'
Gill: every class of Baal's worship — prophets, priests, servants — is summoned.
'Sanctify a solemn meeting' implies each worshipper must ceremonially purify himself; criers proclaim the festival through all Israel.
Gill: the 'solemn assembly' is like a Jewish holy convocation, a work-free day so all may attend.
'Full from one end to another' is a vessel-image, 'brimful,' the LXX's 'mouth to mouth'; a marginal gloss has intruded into the Vatican text.
Gill: Jehu sent criers to proclaim the assembly through all the land, and every worshipper came.

themes: the temple filled to the walls

schools.summary: The 'vestry' is debated — Keil, the Baal temple's own wardrobe of sacred robes; Ellicott, Jehu's order that all wear their distinctive dress at the festival.
Clarke: 'vestry' from vestiarium; Baal's priests had robes easily distinguished from those of the Lord's priests.
Gill: the officer kept the garments in which Baal's priests ministered.
The search reads to us as sinister but to the Baalites as reverence for Baal; eighty guards, answerable with their lives, are posted outside.
Clarke: the search was made on pretext of expelling the profane, as the heathen did, to avert suspicion.
Gill: Jehonadab, entering with Jehu, was clearly let into the secret of the plan.
The officiating priest offers while eighty guards, each answerable with his life, ring the temple; the killing is timed to the worship.
Gill: the worshippers went in to sacrifice, all things ready, unaware it was their death-service.
The blow falls when all are absorbed in worship; 'the city of the house of Baal' is the temple's inner citadel or stronghold, stormed after the massacre in the court.
Clarke: a conscientious man would have finished the killing before sacrificing, but to Jehu Jehovah and Baal are alike; Wesley reads 'the city' as the temple's buildings.
Gill: the worshippers went in to sacrifice, all things ready for the slaughter.
The word means wooden 'pillars' of sacred significance, burned, as distinct from the central stone image of Baal, broken in pieces; the law commanded idolatrous pillars destroyed.
Gill: these were lesser images or decorations of the temple.
The temple is made a 'draught house,' the coarse word softened by the Masoretes, a calculated Oriental insult; 'unto this day' bears on the record's date.
Clarke: a public necessary — nothing could be more degrading.
Gill: the temple is demolished; the deity was probably the sun.
Objectively the destruction of Baal accorded with the Law; subjectively Jehu's motive was selfish policy, proved by his retention of Jeroboam's calves.
Gill: Baal's images and temples were destroyed throughout the whole land of Israel.

themes: a good deed, a selfish motive

schools.summary: Henry: Jehu parted with Baal, which cost him nothing, but kept the calves, a 'politic idolatry' propping his throne; true conversion forsakes gainful sins.
He kept Bethel and Dan on grounds of state policy; 'Howbeit' is the redactor's qualifying 'Only.'
Wesley: his religion was superficial, or it would not have given way to his policy.
Gill: the sin of Jeroboam, 'who made Israel to sin,' is a blot never wiped off.
God's 'thou hast done well' refers to the deed as such, not to Jehu's motive; the promise to the fourth generation is fulfilled in 2 Kings 15:12.
Clarke: the four kings (Jehoahaz to Zechariah) were all Jehu's recompense in either world; Wesley notes he did well only 'in part.'
Gill: the word came by a prophet, said by the Jews to be Jonah son of Amittai.

themes: the deed approved, not the motive

schools.summary: He 'had not been careful' to keep the Mosaic law that forbade the calves; honouring Jehovah against Baal, he kept Jeroboam's state-worship, and so ripened Israel for chastisement.
Clarke: he never made it his study nor intended to walk in the law; it suited neither his disposition nor his politics.
Gill: his obedience was not sincere nor from a right principle.
'To cut Israel short' is to cut off territory part by part; Jehu's Assyrian alliance drew Hazael's hostility, and the loss is the direct consequence of v31's failure.
Clarke: 'cut off the ends' — Hazael seized the frontier, beginning the ravages Elisha foretold.
Gill: Israel was straitened even under so vigorous a king, so that God's hand was the more visible.
The whole trans-Jordan territory of the two and a half tribes, from Aroer on the Arnon through Gilead and Bashan, was conquered by Hazael with great barbarity.
Gill: the region smitten lay east of Canaan, from Jordan eastward.
Clarke: for barbarity and hypocrisy Jehu has few parallels; God decreed that Ahab's house fall, but the cruel manner was permitted, not commanded.
Ellicott: some texts omit 'all his might'; the LXX adds 'and the conspiracies which he conspired.'
Gill: his acts were recorded in the chronicles of the kings of Israel.

debates.question: Did God ordain the bloody means, or only decree the end?

sides.position: God decreed the cutting off of Ahab's house, but the cruel manner was Jehu's own, only permitted

schools.summary: Jehu dies a natural death, is buried in Samaria his capital, and is succeeded by Jehoahaz, the first of the promised four generations.
Gill: 'slept with his fathers' is the formula for dying as they did.
'In Samaria' is placed last, marking it as the seat of Jehu's court; the reign's length is given only at the end because his accession was never formally announced.
Gill: twenty-eight years, longer than any king of Israel had reigned.
